events.description: Amazon officially announced Long Island City, Queens, as one of the two locations for its new headquarters, sparking immediate local debate.
Public reports revealed that New York City and State officials offered Amazon approximately $3 billion in tax incentives to secure the deal.
Local residents and activists held a town hall meeting in Queens to protest the lack of transparency and the potential impact on housing affordability.
Amazon executives testified before the New York City Council, facing intense questioning regarding labor practices and the use of public subsidies.

New York State Senate Majority Leader Andrea Stewart-Cousins appointed vocal Amazon critic Senator Michael Gianaris to the Public Authorities Control Board, giving him potential veto power over the deal.
Amazon officially canceled its plans to build the Long Island City headquarters, citing opposition from local politicians and community members.

polls.methodology: Survey of New York City voters

stakeholders.name: Bill de Blasio
stakeholders.position: He was a primary proponent of the deal, arguing that the headquarters would bring 25,000 jobs and significant economic growth to the city.

stakeholders.name: Andrew Cuomo
stakeholders.position: He strongly championed the Amazon project, framing it as a major victory for New York's economy and a testament to the state's competitiveness.

stakeholders.name: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez
stakeholders.position: She emerged as a vocal critic, arguing that the $3 billion in public subsidies should have been invested in local infrastructure and education rather than a wealthy corporation.

stakeholders.name: Jimmy Van Bramer
stakeholders.position: He opposed the deal, citing concerns over the lack of transparency in the negotiations and the negative impact of tax breaks on the local community.

stakeholders.name: Michael Gianaris
stakeholders.position: He was a leading opponent who criticized the use of public funds for Amazon and questioned the company's labor practices and impact on housing affordability.

outcome_description: Following intense public and political backlash regarding the $3 billion in tax incentives and concerns over gentrification, Amazon officially canceled its plans to build an HQ2 campus in Long Island City, Queens, in February 2019.
outcome_source: Siena College Research Institute (February 2019 poll showing 57% of New York voters supported the project, despite the vocal opposition that led to its cancellation)
